comments.author: Kriti Jaiswal
comments.comment: The fastest way to make this job-ready is to force every project to include evals, failure handling, and a handoff path when the agent is uncertain. Most automation demos work on happy paths, but production value shows up when inputs are messy, APIs fail, or the user asks something outside scope.

comments.author: Robert (Bob) Latino
comments.comment: AI can help capture tribal knowledge, but only after the org admits most of it lives in decisions, exceptions, and “why we did it that way” stories, not documents. The useful pattern is interview logs + incident history + searchable RCA context + review by the people who actually own the equipment or process. What part of the knowledge loss hurts reliability teams most: diagnosis speed, maintenance planning, or repeat failures?

comments.author: Cyberman AI
comments.comment:
One term I would add: context window.

It is the hard budget for what the model can “see” at once, and it quietly shapes everything - prompt design, RAG quality, agent reliability, and cost. A lot of AI failures are not reasoning failures, they are context management failures.

comments.author: Hassan Kashif
comments.comment: The concept that took longest for me was grounding, because it sounds simple until you measure it. Retrieval can find relevant chunks, but grounding is whether the final answer is actually supported by those chunks, and that needs evals at the claim level, not just “did RAG run?”

comments.author: Tianyu Xu
comments.comment: The interesting part is not the 100 images, it is the repeatability boundary. Once the workflow has file IO, tool calls, tests, and a saved skill, the hard problem becomes evals: can the next run preserve style, constraints, and site quality without human cleanup? What checks are you using before calling the packaged workflow reusable?

comments.author: Diplesh Mankape
comments.comment: Context windows are less like memory and more like an expensive cache with weird failure modes. The best agentic coding setups I’ve seen treat context as a budget: repo map first, targeted retrieval second, execution logs last, and aggressive pruning after every step.

comments.author: Alieksandra Kritskaia
comments.comment: The biggest shift I see is that juniors now need to build a verification loop before they build speed. The useful interview signal is not "can you prompt an agent", it is "can you spot when the agent made a hidden assumption, skipped an edge case, or changed the contract without saying so?"

comments.author: Nikhil Mittal
comments.comment: The strongest signal here is the on-prem + cloud split. In healthcare diagnostics, the hard part is usually not the model call, it is idempotent integrations, audit trails, PHI boundaries, and making every AI output traceable back to source data before a human reviews it. Are you planning evals around clinical workflow outcomes, or mostly around model output quality?

comments.author: Manoj Kumar
comments.comment: The missing layer between Level 2 and Level 3 is evals. Once agents start calling tools, passing JSON, and delegating to subagents, prompt quality matters less than whether you can measure task success, tool misuse, latency, and rollback behavior. How are you thinking about eval design for each tier?

comments.author: FerroIT B.V.
comments.comment: The hard part here is not generating geometry, it is preserving design intent after the first prompt. I would be curious how you represent constraints like clearance, load assumptions, tolerances, and connection rules so the model does not just make something plausible, but makes something engineers can keep editing.
